module.title: The Watch at Crow's Hollow
module.author: Lantern & Blade Project
module.description: A derelict Warden watch-house on the Hollow Edge. For three nights running, the lanterns along its curtain wall have relit themselves at dusk and burned until dawn. The nearest village sent a child to investigate; the child did not come back. A short dungeon in six rooms, climaxing in the Warden-Lieutenant's crypt. Designed for a level 3 character operating solo.

watch_house_yard.name: The Watch-House Yard
watch_house_yard.description: The road from Thornford's Common runs out of the woods and right up to the yard's low stone wall, as if it were still an imperial thoroughfare. Beyond the wall: a flagged courtyard strangled with dry grass, a half-collapsed stable, and the watch-house itself — two stories of squat grey limestone with a peaked slate roof. Above the oaken door, a carved crest: a stylized lantern encircled by a chain of laurels. Above the crest, set into sconces along the curtain wall, four iron watch-lanterns burn with a thin, steady, unshielded flame. It is mid-afternoon. There is no wind to speak of. The lanterns are lit anyway.

environment.name: Unquiet Stillness
environment.description: No birds. No insects. The sound of the player's own breath is audible. The lanterns do not flicker.
effects: Sound carries further than it should — the slightest noise from inside the watch-house is likely to be heard.

features.name: The Warden Crest
features.description: A single large stone carved above the oaken door: a lantern wrapped in a chain of laurels. Beneath it, in small deeply-cut script, the Watch's motto — WE KEEP THE LIGHT SO THE LIGHT KEEPS US.
features.on_examine: The laurels are not a Dawnlight convention — they are older, pre-imperial, and the Wardens borrowed them from someone who mattered to the pre-imperial cults. The crest predates the watch-house itself; it's been moved here from somewhere else. Someone is buried under the stone the crest is carved into, or was.
features.gm_notes: Players bearing the Rubbing of the Lantern Tree will recognize the motto instantly. Players bearing the Lanternblade will see the blade's runes warm slightly against the crest.

features.name: The Burning Watch-Lanterns
features.description: Four iron watch-lanterns along the curtain wall, set in bracketed sconces at roughly head height. Each is burning with a steady, pale, unshielded flame about the size of a thumb. There is no oil in them. There is no wick.
features.on_examine: The flames do not respond to breath. A palm brought near one feels warmth, but the warmth does not spread — it is present exactly in the flame and nowhere else. This is proper Warden lantern-work behaving the way Warden lantern-work is supposed to behave. Something inside the watch-house has relit them.
features.gm_notes: The lanterns are a breadcrumb: whatever is in the crypt is waking, and the old lantern-work is responding. Do not let the player extinguish them — they cannot be put out from outside.

gate_room.name: The Gate Room
gate_room.description: The oaken door opens on a high-ceilinged guardroom paved in slate, cold enough that the player's breath shows. A long wooden counter runs along the right wall, its surface worn smooth by a thousand signed duty-rosters. Above it, pegged to a plank, a Warden muster-roll. Two doorways lead off to the left and right; a third, directly ahead, is an iron-strapped door set deeper into the wall than seems structurally necessary. Everything is coated with the fine grey dust of sixty years of emptiness — except a single line of bootprints, fresh, small, leading inward from the threshold and vanishing at the rightmost doorway.

the iron-strapped door.reveal_hint: The iron-strapped door will not open. Its bar-latch is warded with an old lantern-sigil — it is not a mechanical lock.

environment.name: Old Cold
environment.description: The room holds the specific deep chill of a cellar that has not been warmed in decades. Breath shows. Metal is painful to touch ungloved.
effects: Prolonged bare contact with metal risks numb fingers; a GM may call for a Constitution save against extended exposure.

features.name: The Muster-Roll
features.description: A split oak plank pegged to the wall above the counter, roughly two feet wide, bearing a list of fourteen names in the neat column-hand of a Warden quartermaster. The top of the plank reads CROW'S HOLLOW GARRISON · WINTER OF THE SIXTY-THIRD YEAR. Beside each name, a small inked symbol: fourteen of them match — a laurel. One name, second from the top — LT. ORICK HAVEL — has no laurel beside it. Instead, a small circle with a line through it.
features.on_examine: A character with Warden lore or who has read any Warden document before will know: the laurel means 'returned home properly' — a Warden who died was laid to rest under their own name. The circle-with-a-line is the older mark — 'returned, but not properly.' Lt. Orick Havel, whoever he was, is somewhere in this watch-house, and the Watch itself knew he had not been put away correctly.
features.gm_notes: First major clue. This is the player's introduction to the Warden-Lieutenant as a named figure with an unfinished problem.

features.name: Overturned Equipment Locker
features.description: A tall wooden locker behind the counter has been pulled from the wall and dumped on its side. The Warden quartermaster's seal — a small waxy lantern stamp — has been broken off the latch, evidently very recently. Most of what it held is scattered: mothballed gambesons, a pair of broken belt-hooks, and a surprising quantity of grey paper envelopes sealed with a cord.
features.on_success: Among the scattered envelopes is a small oilcloth-wrapped bundle that escaped the thieves' notice: two sealed vials, a folded paper bearing a hand-drawn diagram, and a few iron nails.
features.on_failure: The locker is mostly empty; someone has been through it already.
features.gm_notes: Whoever broke the quartermaster's seal is the same party whose bootprints lead into the officer's study. Foreshadow without naming.

hazards.name: Rotten Floorboard
hazards.description: A single wide board of the slate flooring is, in fact, not slate — it is a rotten plank painted grey to match. It covers an old drainage pit that the original garrison used for washing blood off the slate after a fight.
hazards.gm_notes: Easy detect, medium avoidance. A player who finds it may use the pit tactically later (dragging an enemy in, hiding).

detection.on_success: One of the slate flags rings hollow under the foot. Closer, it's plainly a painted plank. Below: a rectangular pit, maybe four feet deep, dry but for old brown staining.

avoidance.on_success: You spread your weight across the adjacent flags and cross the plank without putting full weight on it.

on_failure.narration: The plank gives all at once, with the sound of a dry branch snapping, and drops you four feet down into a cold stone-walled pit. You land hard on an ankle.

reward_on_detection.narration: You keep your footing and note the pit for later.

barracks.name: The Barracks
barracks.description: A long, low-ceilinged hall lined with six double-stacked wooden bunks and a single scarred trestle table down the middle. On the table: a tin cup, a deck of grey-backed playing cards fanned out mid-game, and — still in the small wooden bowl beside it — a pile of copper pennies representing somebody's lost hand. Nobody has finished this game yet. The air here is colder than the gate room. Not one of the bunks has its blankets pulled back.

environment.name: Wrong Cold
environment.description: The temperature drops visibly as the player enters — breath shows immediately, the cards on the table stir as if in a draft that is not there.
effects: Living creatures in the room have disadvantage on saves against fear-inducing effects while the Private's spirit is active.

features.name: The Unfinished Hand
features.description: The cards on the trestle table are laid out mid-hand. Three players; all three hands visible, which is not how the game is played. The pile of pennies sits in the middle, untouched. The topmost card has been dealt face-down on top of the pile, as if the dealer had been interrupted exactly then.
features.on_examine: The cards are Warden quartermaster's issue — grey backs with a small lantern printed in the corner. Nothing about the game is unusual except that all three hands are visible and nobody has collected the penny-pile.
features.gm_notes: If the restless spirit encounter fires, the GM should narrate the spirit sitting down to deal the missing fourth card before attacking, or similar pathos.

features.name: The Kit Racks
features.description: Along the back wall, six narrow kit racks — one per bunk — each hung with what its occupant left behind. Most of the racks have been stripped by sixty years of rust and rodents. Two are mostly intact, their contents sealed in oilcloth wrappings.
features.on_success: You find an oilcloth-wrapped bundle containing a serviceable torch, fifty feet of good hempen rope, and a small glass vial of grey-green antitoxin.
features.on_failure: The surviving bundles turn out to hold only mothballed cloth and a few rusted buckles.
features.prereq_hint: The racks are behind the bunks at the far end of the room — it would be rash to turn your back on whatever is in here to go rummaging.

encounters.name: The Unfinished Hand
encounters.placement: The restless spirit rises from the trestle-table side, reaching for the undealt card. The two skeleton warriors sit up simultaneously in adjacent bunks, the bunks creaking under the motion.
encounters.behavior: The skeletons fight mindlessly and pursue the nearest living thing. The spirit attacks only targets who handled the cards, the pennies, or the table; it leaves alone anyone who simply crosses the room to the kit racks.
encounters.gm_notes: Bludgeoning damage fells the skeletons quickly (they have vulnerability). The spirit is the real problem: resistant to non-magical weapons, so a level-3 party without the Lanternblade or the wand should expect attrition. A clever player may realize the spirit calms if the dealt card is turned over and the hand finished — prose-adjudicate this as a full resolution if the player reasons it out.

officers_study.name: The Officer's Study
officers_study.description: A small square room lined on three walls with narrow shelves of Warden ledgers and bound codices, most of them intact but bloated with damp. The fourth wall is a single tall window of iron-bound glass, leaking grey afternoon light. In the middle of the room: a writing-desk of dark wood, its drawer pulled partly open, and the evidence of a very recent, very frustrated search — drawer tipped, paper scattered, a cold ash-ring on the floor where a small camp-lantern has burned out. On the desk itself, a heavy iron-bound lockbox, its hasp sealed by a softly-glowing lantern-sigil about the size of a thumbprint.

features.name: The Warden-Lieutenant's Journal
features.description: Beneath the mess the goblins made, still partially hidden under the drawer's false bottom, is a small leather-bound book. The ink is faded; the hand is Lt. Orick Havel's, the same as the signature on the muster-roll.
features.on_success:
You find the false bottom on the second pass of the drawer. The journal is written in the plain column-hand of a Warden officer and covers the last two weeks of the Falling Winter. The final entry, undated:

"The thing from the Blackfall broke through the ward at Thornford's Common and we pursued. Caught it at the crypt beneath this post. My company is dead. I am bleeding out as I write. I cannot reach the crypt door — the seal requires the closing phrase, and my voice is going. If a Warden finds this, the phrase is engraved on the inside of my cloak-pin. Speak it at the Wardstone Lantern and the crypt will open. Finish what I began. Light the lantern. Speak the phrase: WE KEEP THE LIGHT, AND THE LIGHT KEEPS US. Burn me. Do not leave me unfinished. — Orick"
features.on_failure: Paper, paper, paper — and no journal. Maybe next pass.
features.prereq_hint: There are goblins in here. Searching the desk properly means dealing with them first.

features.name: The Warden's Lockbox
features.description: A heavy iron-bound lockbox on the desk. No hinges, no visible seam. A single small lantern-sigil glows softly on the lid, warm to the touch, its light too steady to be a fire.

solution.description: The lockbox is sealed with a proper Warden lantern-sigil. It will open to any Warden who speaks the Watch's motto aloud, and to anyone who can convincingly invoke the Light. Accept any utterance of 'We keep the light so the light keeps us' or a clear declaration of Warden allegiance. The sigil responds to intent as much as to words — a bearer of the Lanternblade or of the Rubbing of the Lantern Tree will find the box opens for them with minimal prompting.

on_success.narration: The lantern-sigil brightens, steadies, and flows out across the lid in four thin lines of light that meet at the corners. The lid rises on its own. Inside: a folded square of oilcloth wrapped around a single rolled parchment, and a small silver cloak-pin shaped like a lantern in laurels.

on_failure.narration: The sigil dims disapprovingly and does not respond further. The box remains sealed.

features.gm_notes: Pure-narrative bypass is the intended path; a player quoting the motto auto-succeeds. The arcana check is a fallback for players who don't make the connection. The cloak-pin itself is flavor — no mechanical effect in v1, but note it for players who carry it (villagers on return may recognize them as Warden-adjacent).

encounters.name: Goblin Scavengers
encounters.placement: Two scouts duck behind the overturned drawer and the window as the player enters; the brute, who had been crowbarring the lockbox without success, turns with the crowbar still in hand and bellows for his fellows.
encounters.behavior: The brute commits immediately with Savage Rush on the first round; scouts open with shortbows from cover and use Nimble Escape to disengage if the player closes. If the brute falls, remaining scouts break morale narratively — run for the gate room and the door. They do not return.
encounters.gm_notes: First proper combat. The goblins are here because they followed the reopened seal's glow from the forest; they are out of their depth. If one flees to the gate room and makes it outside, treat the watch-house's lanterns as extinguishing briefly as it crosses the yard — small narrative beat, the Wardens of the Light are still doing their job in their own way.

module_bestiary.name: Crow's Hollow — Module Foes
module_bestiary.description: Unique foes specific to The Watch at Crow's Hollow.
